figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overview of REALM’s cross-modal versatility. By mapping a sparse event stream into DUNE’s [49] latent space, our model enables diverse downstream applications. This includes feature matching using the frozen MASt3R [36] decoder and dense prediction tasks like semantic segmentation and depth estimation using simple linear heads.

Fig. 2: Overview of the cross-modal distillation framework. Event representations undergo masked autoencoders (MAE)-style patch dropout before being processed by a trainable embedding layer and a LoRA-adapted student encoder. The network is trained to match the latent representation of the frozen DUNE teacher. A progressive spatial mask is applied to the distillation loss (L) to focus the alignment on regions with active event data, preventing background overfitting.
Fig. 4: Qualitative depth estimation results on the MVSEC [66] dataset.

Table 1: Quantitative results on the MVSEC [66] dataset. To train the linear prediction head, data from DENSE [29] and MVSEC [66], following the training protocol of e2depth [29]. We report results for DUNE [49] with frame-based inputs as a reference, and for our event-only method, REALM. Comparisons are provided against supervised approaches [29], as well as unsupervised methods [67,68].

Table 4: Pose estimation performance grouped by sensing modality. Higher AUC and success rate are better, while a lower median angular error is better. The experiments are conducted on the VECtor [18] dataset.
Table 3: Quantitative results on the ECD [42] and EDS [28] datasets. We report the Area Under Curve (AUC) in % at different thresholds.
Fig. 5: Qualitative semantic segmentation on the DSEC [22] driving dataset.

Fig. 6: Cross-modal and intra-modal feature matching. We report the AUC at 5◦ per scene and visualize the correspondence estimation results for inliers and outliers in green and orange respectively. REALM uses the pre-trained MASt3R [36] decoder in a zero-shot manner without any further fine-tuning on event data. For comparison, we also report the matching performance of MINIMA [47].
